metadata.summary: Quick-reference for PMBoK 7/8 domains, principles, EVM formulae, estimation accuracy bands, risk strategies, RAG thresholds. Ground-truth LLM outputs.

content.title: PMBoK 7 and 8 Reference
content.summary: Quick-reference tables for PMBoK 7 (8 performance domains, 12 principles) and PMBoK 8 (7 domains, 6 principles), plus EVM formulae, estimation accuracy bands, risk response strategies, and RAG status thresholds. This is a constants module for agent system prompts — inject the relevant table to anchor terminology. Not a methodology for doing anything; pair with operational methodologies (WBS, risk register, stakeholder engagement).

why.p: PMBoK 6 (process groups + knowledge areas) is heavily over-represented in LLM training data. Agents default to "Integration Management" and "Quality Management" as domain names even when instructed to use PMBoK 7/8. Injecting the exact table from this reference into the system prompt forces the right vocabulary. EVM arithmetic on more than 5 rows drifts in LLMs — always compute in code.

list.item: Ground-truthing PMBoK terminology in system prompts, sponsor decks, or certification content.
Quick-lookup of EVM formulae, risk strategies, estimation accuracy, RAG thresholds during status report generation.
Disambiguating PMBoK 6 vs 7 vs 8 vocabulary before generating any PM content.
Building a translation layer for teams migrating from one edition to another.
As a standalone methodology to do anything — it is a reference; pair with operational methodologies.
For non-PMI frameworks (PRINCE2, IPMA, ISO 21500) — vocabulary overlaps but differs; a pure PMBoK lens distorts those frameworks.
For agile-only environments that have no baseline — EVM formulae are meaningless without a scope and cost baseline.

section.name: pmbok7-performance-domains
section.p: PMBoK 7 defines eight performance domains that cover the entire project management landscape. These domains replace the outdated process-group model from PMBoK 6.

list.item: Stakeholder — engaging stakeholders; outcome: agreement on objectives and sustained support.
Team — building high-performing teams; outcome: shared ownership and growth.
Development Approach — selecting predictive/agile/hybrid; outcome: right approach for context.
Planning — scope, schedule, cost, resources; outcome: realistic and achievable plans.
Project Work — executing and monitoring; outcome: efficient delivery.
Delivery — value and outcomes; outcome: benefits realised.
Measurement — tracking with metrics; outcome: data-driven decisions.
Uncertainty — risks and complexity; outcome: managed uncertainty.

section.name: pmbok7-twelve-principles
section.p: PMBoK 7 identifies twelve foundational principles that should guide how projects are managed. These principles cut across all domains and provide the philosophical foundation for project management practice.

list.item: P-01 Stewardship: act with integrity, be accountable.
P-02 Team: build culture of accountability and respect.
P-03 Stakeholders: engage proactively.
P-04 Value: focus on outcomes, not outputs.
P-05 Systems Thinking: see the whole, understand interdependencies.
P-06 Leadership: inspire, adapt style, enable others.
P-07 Tailoring: adapt processes to context.
P-08 Quality: build quality into processes.
P-09 Complexity: navigate with appropriate strategies.
P-10 Risk: balance risk with reward.
P-11 Adaptability: embrace change, learn from experience.
P-12 Change: enable envisioned future state.

section.name: evm-quick-reference
section.p: Earned Value Management (EVM) is a quantitative method for measuring project performance against an approved scope and cost baseline. The following formulae are the standard definitions used in PMBoK and PMI certifications. Always compute EVM metrics in code, never by hand — LLMs drift on arithmetic.

rule.statement: Always show formula AND result when reporting EVM metrics. Never emit only the computed value — auditors need the formula to verify the calculation.
rule.rationale: Audit trails require reproducibility. A bare "$92K EAC" without formula cannot be verified or challenged.

rule.statement: Clamp EVM denominators to avoid division by zero. When PV or AC is zero, emit null for the affected metrics rather than infinity or NaN.
rule.rationale: LLM arithmetic on EVM rows with zero or near-zero denominators produces nonsense results that look plausible. Code calculation with explicit guards is required.

list.item: SV (Schedule Variance) = EV - PV; positive = ahead of schedule.
CV (Cost Variance) = EV - AC; positive = under budget.
SPI (Schedule Performance Index) = EV / PV; greater than 1 = ahead.
CPI (Cost Performance Index) = EV / AC; greater than 1 = under budget.
EAC (Estimate at Completion) = BAC / CPI.
ETC (Estimate to Complete) = EAC - AC.
VAC (Variance at Completion) = BAC - EAC.
BAC (Budget at Completion) — total approved budget.
PV (Planned Value) — budget for work scheduled to be done by now.
EV (Earned Value) — budget for work actually completed so far.
AC (Actual Cost) — cost of work actually completed so far.

section.name: estimation-accuracy-bands
section.p: PMBoK defines four estimation techniques with characteristic accuracy bands. The technique used must be named alongside the band; the band is not a guarantee but a heuristic based on the technique's historical performance.

list.item: Analogous: -50% to +100% — early stages, rough order of magnitude.
Parametric: -10% to +25% — historical data available.
Bottom-up: -5% to +10% — detailed scope defined, most accurate.
Three-point (PERT): variable — risk-aware, useful with high uncertainty.

rule.statement: Present estimation accuracy bands as heuristics with ranges, never as guarantees. An agent that quotes "+/- 10%" without "bottom-up only" misleads sponsors.
rule.rationale: Sponsors anchor on the tightest accuracy band regardless of which technique was used. The technique must be named alongside the band.

section.name: risk-response-strategies
section.p: PMBoK defines four response strategies for threats and four for opportunities. Every identified risk must be paired with exactly one response strategy in the risk register.

list.item: Threats: Avoid (eliminate), Mitigate (reduce probability/impact), Transfer (shift to third party), Accept (acknowledge and monitor).
Opportunities: Exploit (ensure occurrence), Enhance (increase probability/impact), Share (partner to capture), Accept (take if occurs).

rule.statement: Always pair a risk entry with exactly one response strategy. A risk without a strategy is an observation, not a managed risk.
rule.rationale: Risk registers that lack strategies are decoration. The strategy and owner are what make a risk register actionable in execution.

section.name: rag-status
section.p: Red-Amber-Green (RAG) status is a simple three-level traffic light used to communicate project health at a glance. RAG inflation is a common problem — when most projects are amber, the threshold is miscalibrated.

list.item: Green: on track — continue monitoring.
Amber: at risk — corrective action plan required within 48 hours.
Red: off track — escalation required immediately.

rule.statement: If more than 50% of projects in a portfolio are amber, the RAG threshold is miscalibrated, not the projects. Recalibrate before acting on individual project statuses.
rule.rationale: RAG inflation (everyone is amber) destroys the signal. Amber must be rare enough that it triggers genuine attention.

section.name: pmbok8-core-changes
section.p: PMBoK 8 (published 2024) introduces significant changes from PMBoK 7. Awareness of these changes is essential to avoid edition drift in agent outputs and certification study materials.

list.item: Principles: 12 (PMBoK 7) streamlined to 6 (PMBoK 8): Stewardship and Ethics, Collaboration, Value Optimisation, Systems and Complexity, Adaptability, Sustainability.
Performance Domains: 8 (PMBoK 7) reorganised to 7 (PMBoK 8).
Sustainability: moved from "mentioned" in PMBoK 7 to a core principle in PMBoK 8; requires a measurable proxy (CO2 per release, accessibility audit pass rate).
AI in PM: no coverage in PMBoK 7; dedicated appendix in PMBoK 8 covering schedule prediction, risk analysis, resource optimisation, status reporting.
Processes: none (fully principle-based in PMBoK 7) to 40 non-prescriptive processes in PMBoK 8.
Quality: integrated throughout all domains in both editions; never a standalone domain.
Communications: merged into Stakeholders domain in PMBoK 8.
Procurement: moved to appendix in PMBoK 8; no longer a standalone domain.

section.name: agent-edition-drift-rules
section.p: LLMs trained on PMBoK 6 materials continue to emit PMBoK 6 vocabulary even when instructed to use PMBoK 7 or 8. These rules help detect and prevent edition drift.

rule.statement: Pin the PMBoK edition in every project charter and in every agent system prompt that generates PM content. Never let an agent assume the edition.
rule.rationale: PMBoK 6 is over-represented in training data. Without explicit pinning, agents default to the 6th edition vocabulary (knowledge areas, process groups) regardless of instruction.

antipattern.description: Agent output contains "Integration Management", "Cost Management", or "Communications Management" as domain names.
antipattern.reason: These are PMBoK 6 knowledge area names. In PMBoK 7/8 these are replaced by Governance, Finance, and integrated Stakeholders respectively. Output containing these names must be regenerated with PMBoK 7/8 vocabulary pinned.

antipattern.description: Agent applies EVM metrics to a pure-agile project with no scope or cost baseline.
antipattern.reason: EVM requires a baseline. Without one, SPI and CPI are undefined. Agile metrics (cycle time, flow efficiency, throughput) must be used instead.

antipattern.description: "RAG" used without spelling out Red/Amber/Green on first use in PM contexts.
antipattern.reason: "RAG" is also retrieval-augmented generation. In PM system prompts, abbreviation without disambiguation causes agents to hallucinate RAG retrieval references in status reports.

template.purpose: EVM calculator: SV, CV, SPI, CPI, EAC, ETC, VAC, RAG from BAC/PV/EV/AC inputs
